content.full_text:
Farn Por-rucar. PRACrrcBs C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
May 24,2002
John Foster
1025 Hickory Avenue
Fairfield, CA94533
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-02-125
Dear Mr. Foster.
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").'
QUESTTONS
l. 
Does the doctrine of incompatible offices bar you from holding office as a member
of the Solano County Airport Land Use Commission ("ALUC") and also serving on the
City of Fairfield's Planning Commission ("Planning Commission")?
2. If you volunteer to serve on the Planning Commission, will you have a conflict of
interest disqualifying you from participating in the decisions of the ALUC or the
Planning Commission?
CONCLUSIONS
1. There is nothing in the Act that prohibits you from holding two public positions
concurrently, although other laws outside the jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices
Commission ("Commission") may restrict the ability of a public official such as yourself
from holding two public offices simultaneously, if those offices are determined to be
"incompatible" of{ices. You are urged to check with the state Attorney General's office
or the city attorney's office of your jurisdiction to determine if there are other laws that
may be applicable in light of the facts you present.
I Government Code sections 81000 - glOI4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 1 09 - I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.
t::


File No. A-02-T25
Page No. 2
2. Holding positions on both the ALUC and the Planning Commission does not, in
itself create a potential disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. However, under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, as a member of either public body, you may
not make, participate in making, or influence the governmental decisions of either public
body when to do so will have a foreseeable, material financial effect on one or more of
your economic interests.
FACTS
You are an existing member of the ALUC, which also acts as the Solano County
Airport Advisory Committee. You were appointed to that position by a member of the
Solano County Board of Supervisors. You would also like to volunteer to serye on the
Planning Commission, whose members are appointed by the mayor of Fairfield. These
two commissions share similar roles at times, in that they both occasionally review and
decide land use issues around airports. Travis Air Force Base is within both
commission's area of responsibility. In this regard we are enclosing a fact sheet prepared
by the commission and entitled "Holding Two Positions" that may be of further
assistance to you.
ANALYSIS
Incompatible O.ffices
The Commission's advice is limited to matters arising under the Act. (Regulation
18329(bX8)(D) ) Other laws do restrict the ability of public officials to hold two
different public offices simultaneously if the offices are determine to be "incompatible
offices." It would be advisable for you to contact the state Attorney General's office with
respect to the doctrine of incompatible offices and other provisions of law that might
apply to your facts.
Conflict of Interest
As an appointed member to the ACLU and, if appointed, to the planning
Commission, you are a "public official" within the meaning of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a)(1).) The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions disqualify a public
official from making, participating in making or influencing governmental decisions that
can reasonably be foreseen as having a material financial effect upon the public official's
economic interests. (Section 87100;regulation 18700(a).) Public officials, therefore, do
not have a conflict of interest on general principles; a conflict of interest arises, rf at all,
in context of the official's involvement in governmental decisions that affect the official's
economic interests. Holding positions on both the ALUC and the Planning Commission
does not, in itself, create a potential disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest (regulation 18700, subdivisions (bXl)-
(b)(8)), that is discussed in the enclosed commission pamphlet,"canl vote?" This


File No. A-02-125
Page No. 3
information should inform you whether you may have a conflict of interest disqualifying
you from being involved in a specific governmental decision. You may also request our
written advice. In this latter regard, the Commission does not offer advice on past
conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8XB) ) Consequently, should you request our written
advice on a specific decision, that request must be made in advance of the decision, and
with sufficient time for an analysis of your question and preparation of a response.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
L. Glick
Legal

interest disqualifying you from participating in the decisions of the ALUC or the
Planning Commission?
